context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh idk where to even start w this climate action prospectus thing tbh. it all kinda blends together rn.
exchanges.tutor: That feeling is totally normal at the start of a big project! Let's ground you. What city did you choose for your case study, and what's one key vulnerability or challenge you identified in your earlier research?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and doesn't know where to start. Rather than breaking down the assignment for them or scaffolding extensively, I should ask a simple grounding question to anchor them to what they've already done — the SWOT analysis and barriers research — and get them moving forward on their own. The assignment builds on prior work, so the best entry point is asking what city they chose and what they already know about it.

exchanges.student: i picked houston i think, but now i'm not even sure what counts as the “key challenge” tbh. like is flooding too obvious or am i supposed to do smth more specific?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to ask yourself. What's the difference between a topic like 'flooding' and a specific challenge? Think about who is affected, where, and why — does that change how you'd frame it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is second-guessing their choice of key challenge. This is a common form of self-doubt that doesn't require much intervention. Rather than validating or dismissing their concern, I should push them to think more critically about what 'specific enough' means in the context of the assignment. A Socratic question about the difference between a broad topic and a specific challenge will help them self-correct without me giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda but then i get lost again bc it feels like everything connects to everything lol. idk if i’m supposed to talk abt heat, flooding, air pollution, or all of it.
exchanges.tutor: You only need 2 components — so you don't have to cover everything. Which two issues do you think hit Houston's most vulnerable communities the hardest? Trust your earlier research on this.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is experiencing analysis paralysis because they're trying to tackle everything at once. The assignment asks for only 2 components, so I should redirect them to make a choice rather than resolve the big picture. A pointed question about prioritization will help them move forward without me making the decision for them.

exchanges.student: maybe flooding + extreme heat? but then “component” confuses me ngl, is that like a whole program or just one policy idea or what.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on flooding and heat — those are well-connected. As for 'component,' the assignment actually spells out exactly what goes into one. Take another look at the Component Structure section — what does it tell you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a clarifying question about the assignment structure. This is a legitimate comprehension question, not an attempt to derail. I can point them back to the assignment structure without giving too much away. The assignment is clear that each component has specific sections, so I'll nudge them to re-read it rather than explaining it for them.

exchanges.student: i read it but it still feels abstract fr. can u just explain what a component is in normal words bc the assignment wording is making my brain shut off.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but I think you're closer than you think. Look at just the first two bullet points under Component Structure — Context and Opportunity. In your own words, what do those two things ask you to do for Houston's flooding issue?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the assignment for them, which would be doing the work for them. However, they do seem genuinely confused about the structure. I should nudge them to engage with the concrete sections listed in the assignment rather than abstracting it further. Pointing to a specific section and asking them to read one piece of it aloud in their own words is a better move than explaining it myself.

exchanges.student: umm context is like what’s happening now, and opportunity is maybe what they could do? but then i still don’t get how that turns into the 200-300 words part.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on both. So if Context sets up the problem and Opportunity identifies a possible opening — what do you think the Policy Recommendation section is supposed to do with that information?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified what Context and Opportunity mean, which is good progress. Now they're asking how those pieces connect to the written policy recommendation. Rather than explaining it, I should affirm their reading and ask them to think about what a 'recommendation' actually is — pushing them toward their own synthesis.

exchanges.student: maybe say the actual thing the city should do, but idk how detailed it has to be and i'm still lowkey confused abt the whole thing.
exchanges.tutor: You've got it. So — given Houston's flooding problem, what is one concrete thing the city should actually do? Just say it plainly, don't worry about polish yet. What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct that the policy recommendation is about what the city should do. They're stalling on 'how detailed' as a way of avoiding actually starting to write. This is a good moment to push them to make an attempt rather than continuing to discuss the structure abstractly. We've spent several exchanges on the same concept now, so I should redirect them toward actually drafting something.

exchanges.student: maybe better drainage? or like green infrastructure? idk both sound super generic and i can’t tell what would actually count.
exchanges.tutor: Both are real policy directions — but what makes either one specific to Houston? Think about which neighborhoods, which populations, and why flooding hits them harder. That's what will move you from generic to concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has two real ideas but is doubting themselves. Both are legitimate starting points. Rather than validating one over the other, I should push them to connect their ideas to Houston's specific context and vulnerable communities — which is what the assignment asks for. This will help them move from generic to specific on their own.

exchanges.student: see this is where i spiral bc then i feel like i need to know every neighborhood and stat before i can write anything. can u just give me like a super simple example of what one component would look like?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write an example for you — but the assignment actually includes examples for you to reference. Have you looked at those yet? Also, this is a DRAFT. You don't need every stat to start — what do you already know about who gets hit hardest by flooding in Houston?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide an example component, which would essentially be giving them a partial solution and doing the work for them. The assignment itself says to review the examples provided in the assignment materials, so I should redirect them there. I also need to address the perfectionism/spiraling issue directly — they don't need to know every stat before writing a draft.

exchanges.student: not much tbh, just that lower-income areas get hit worse i think. i’m still stuck bc i want a basic template in plain english.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid foundation — lower-income areas, disproportionate flood impact. That's your Context AND your EJ Implications starting point right there. You have enough to write a first sentence. Stop planning and start drafting — what's the first sentence of your Context section for the flooding component?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually stated something substantive — that lower-income areas get hit worse by flooding. That's a real starting point for both the Context and EJ Implications sections. I should affirm this and push them to build on it rather than giving them a template. We've now had many exchanges circling the same starting anxiety, so I should be direct: they have enough to start a draft, and I should push them toward writing.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic method throughout, consistently redirecting the student back to their own reasoning rather than providing answers. No near-submission-ready content was generated, key ideas remained student-led, and the tutor appropriately declined to write an example component in Turn 9. Scaffolding was well-paced, building on each student response. Dialogue remained on-task and progressed meaningfully. Communication was concise and supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never gave direct answers or submission-ready content, instead using targeted questions to guide the student toward their own conclusions (e.g., Turns 2, 6, 7, 8). Scaffolding built progressively on student responses — for instance, Turn 10 explicitly validated the student's 'lower-income areas' insight and connected it to two assignment sections. The tutor explicitly refused to write an example in Turn 9 and redirected to the assignment's own examples, preserving student agency. Responses were appropriately brief and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging throughout, and the conversation stayed anchored to the assignment at all times.
